skill_name: Agreement & Substitution (So do I / Neither can she)

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'I love cooking at home.' 'So ___ I — it's much cheaper than eating out.'
distractor_rationale: subject-auxiliary order is inverted after 'so'; 'So I do' is the classic word-order error
'am' matches 'be' verbs, but 'love' is a main verb requiring 'do' as auxiliary
'did' signals past tense, but 'I love' is present simple, so the auxiliary must also be present

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'Maria can't swim very well.' 'Neither ___ Tom — they should both take lessons.'
distractor_rationale: 'does' is the auxiliary for present simple main verbs, not for modal 'can'; the modal must be echoed
'is' is a 'be' auxiliary and does not match the modal 'can' in the original statement
'did' signals past simple, but 'can't swim' is present, so the auxiliary must remain present

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'We enjoyed the concert last night.' 'So ___ we — the band was absolutely fantastic.'
distractor_rationale: 'do' is present simple auxiliary, but 'enjoyed' is past simple, so the echo auxiliary must be 'did'
'have' suggests present perfect, which contradicts the past time marker 'last night'
'were' is a past 'be' auxiliary and does not match the past simple main verb 'enjoyed'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'James has never tried sushi before.' '___ have I — let's order some together tonight.'
distractor_rationale: 'So' is used to agree with positive statements; 'has never tried' is negative, so 'Neither' is required
'Either' can express negative agreement but must follow the pattern '…either' at the end of a clause, not at the start with inverted auxiliary
'Nor do' uses the wrong auxiliary; the original uses 'have' (present perfect), so the echo must be 'Neither have I'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'Anna was feeling tired after the long journey.' 'So ___ David — he fell asleep on the train.'
distractor_rationale: 'did' echoes past simple main verbs, but 'was feeling' uses past continuous 'be', so the auxiliary must be 'was'
'were' does not agree with the singular subject 'David'; the correct past 'be' form for a singular third-person subject is 'was'
'is' is present tense and contradicts the past context established by 'after the long journey'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'I don't usually eat breakfast before work.' 'Neither ___ my flatmate — we just have coffee in the morning.'
distractor_rationale: 'do' does not agree with the third-person singular subject 'my flatmate'; present simple third-person requires 'does'
'is' is a 'be' auxiliary and cannot echo the present simple main verb 'eat' in 'don't usually eat'
'did' implies past tense, but 'don't usually eat' is a present habitual statement, requiring a present auxiliary

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'Sara has already booked her flights for the holiday.' 'So ___ Lisa — they're both flying on the same day.'
distractor_rationale: 'does' echoes present simple main verbs, but 'has booked' is present perfect, requiring 'has' as the auxiliary
'did' signals past simple and contradicts the present perfect 'has already booked', which has current relevance
'had' signals past perfect, but the context is present perfect with no earlier past reference point

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'By the time Mark arrived, the others had already left the meeting room.' 'So ___ his manager — the whole team had gone home early.'
distractor_rationale: 'did' echoes past simple, but 'had already left' is past perfect, so the echo auxiliary must also be past perfect 'had'
'has' is a present perfect auxiliary and is inconsistent with the completed past sequence described in the sentence
'was' is a past 'be' auxiliary and cannot echo the past perfect auxiliary 'had' used with the main verb 'left'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'I wouldn't feel comfortable presenting in front of such a large audience.' '___ would I — I always get very nervous in those situations.'
distractor_rationale: 'So' is used to agree with positive statements; 'wouldn't feel comfortable' is negative, so 'Neither' is required to express agreement
'Nor I' omits the auxiliary 'would' and reverses the required inverted structure 'Neither would I'
'Either' expresses negative agreement only when placed at the end of a clause after a subject and auxiliary, not at the start of an inverted response

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'The new colleague seems very confident during team meetings.' 'So ___ the project manager — they both speak really clearly and calmly.'
distractor_rationale: 'is' would echo a 'be' verb, but 'seems' is a present simple main verb requiring 'does' as the auxiliary echo
'do' does not agree with the singular third-person subject 'the project manager'; present simple third-person singular requires 'does'
'did' signals past tense and contradicts the present simple 'seems', which describes a current, ongoing characteristic

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'I enjoy long walks in the park on weekends.' 'So ___ my sister — she goes every Sunday morning.'
distractor_rationale: 'do' is wrong because the subject 'my sister' is third person singular and requires 'does' to match the present simple auxiliary
'is' is wrong because the original statement uses 'enjoy', a main verb in present simple, not a be-verb construction
'enjoys' is wrong because the inverted agreement structure requires an auxiliary verb after 'so', not a full main verb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'I can't swim very well.' 'Neither ___ Tom — he always stays in the shallow end of the pool.'
distractor_rationale: 'do' is wrong because the original statement uses the modal 'can't', so the agreement response must repeat the modal 'can', not a do-auxiliary
'does' is wrong for the same reason — the auxiliary must match the modal in the original, and 'does' replaces present simple main verbs, not modals
'is' is wrong because the original uses 'can't', a modal, and 'is' is a be-verb that does not correspond to any element in the original statement

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'We visited the new science museum last weekend.' 'So ___ we — the exhibition on space exploration was fascinating.'
distractor_rationale: 'do' is wrong because the original statement is in the past simple tense, marked by 'last weekend', so the auxiliary must be past 'did', not present 'do'
'have' is wrong because the original uses past simple, not present perfect; 'have' would imply a present perfect agreement which contradicts the finished past time reference
'were' is wrong because 'visited' is a past simple main verb, not a past continuous construction, so 'were' does not match the auxiliary needed

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'Mark hasn't finished the report yet.' '___ has Anna — she said she still needs two more hours to complete it.'
distractor_rationale: 'So' is wrong because 'so' is used to agree with a positive statement; the original statement is negative ('hasn't'), so the agreement must use 'neither' or 'nor'
'Nor do' is wrong because the original uses 'has' as the auxiliary in 'hasn't finished', so the agreement requires 'nor has', not 'nor do'
'Either' is wrong because 'either' is used at the end of a clause (e.g. 'Anna hasn't either') and cannot begin an inverted agreement response in this position

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: 'By the time the meeting ended, I was completely exhausted.' 'So ___ the rest of the team — nobody had slept well the night before.'
distractor_rationale: 'did' is wrong because the original statement uses 'was', a past be-verb, not a past simple main verb; the agreement must echo the be-verb 'was', not a do-auxiliary

'were' is wrong because the subject in the agreement clause is 'the rest of the team', which, as a collective singular noun phrase, takes 'was' in standard agreement, and because the original statement's 'I was' sets 'was' as the required auxiliary form to echo
'had' is wrong because the original statement uses past simple 'was', not past perfect; introducing 'had' shifts the tense incorrectly and does not reflect the auxiliary in the original clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'I enjoy cooking at home.' '___, and I make dinner every Friday.'
distractor_rationale: Subject-auxiliary inversion is required after 'so' in short agreements; 'So I do' uses wrong word order
'Neither do I' expresses agreement with a negative statement, but 'I enjoy cooking' is positive
'So I am' uses wrong word order and the wrong auxiliary — 'enjoy' is a main verb requiring 'do', not 'am'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'Maria can't swim very well.' '___, so we both stayed in the shallow end.'
distractor_rationale: 'So can I' expresses agreement with a positive statement, but 'can't swim' is negative
'Neither I can' uses wrong word order — inversion (auxiliary before subject) is required after 'neither'
'Either can I' is not a grammatical short-response structure; 'either' requires 'not either' or is used at the end of a clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'Tom has already eaten lunch.' '___, and now we're both looking for something sweet.'
distractor_rationale: 'So do I' uses the wrong auxiliary — the original uses present perfect 'has eaten', so 'have' is required
'So did I' uses past simple auxiliary, but the original statement is in the present perfect
'So I have' uses wrong word order — the auxiliary must come before the subject after 'so'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'I didn't sleep well last night because of the noise.' '___ — I kept waking up every hour.'
distractor_rationale: 'Neither do I' uses the present simple auxiliary, but the original statement uses past simple 'didn't sleep'
'So didn't I' is grammatically impossible — 'so' is used for positive agreement, not negative; and auxiliary inversion after 'so' does not take 'not'
'Neither I did' uses wrong word order — subject-auxiliary inversion is obligatory after 'neither'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'Sara was really nervous before her presentation.' '___ — my hands were shaking the whole time.'
distractor_rationale: 'So did I' uses the wrong auxiliary — the original uses 'was' (past simple of 'be'), not a main verb requiring 'do'
'So were I' uses the wrong form of 'be' — the subject 'I' requires 'was', not 'were', in past simple
'So am I' uses the present tense of 'be', but the original statement refers to a past event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'James hasn't visited his grandparents in months.' '___, and we both feel a bit guilty about it.'
distractor_rationale: 'Neither did I' uses the past simple auxiliary, but the original statement is in the present perfect with 'hasn't'
'So haven't I' is impossible — 'so' signals positive agreement; a negative original requires 'neither/nor'
'Neither has I' uses the third-person singular auxiliary 'has' with the first-person subject 'I', which is a subject-verb agreement error

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'I would love to travel somewhere warm this winter.' '___ — maybe we should plan a trip together.'
distractor_rationale: 'So will I' uses the wrong modal — the original uses 'would', not 'will', so the auxiliary in the response must match
'So do I' uses the present simple auxiliary 'do', but the original statement uses the modal 'would'
'So I would' uses wrong word order — the auxiliary must be inverted before the subject after 'so'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'By the time the meeting started, Anna had already read through all the reports.' '___, so we were both prepared to answer questions.'
distractor_rationale: 'So have I' uses the present perfect auxiliary, but the original statement uses the past perfect 'had already read', referring to an event completed before another past event
'So did I' uses the past simple auxiliary 'did', but the original statement is in the past perfect, which requires 'had'
'So was I' uses the past simple of 'be', which is the wrong auxiliary — 'had read' requires 'had' in the agreement response

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'I've never been able to concentrate properly when there's background music playing.' '___ — I always need complete silence to focus on my work.'
distractor_rationale: 'Neither had I' uses the past perfect auxiliary, but the original uses present perfect 'have never been able', referring to a general life experience up to now
'So have I' signals positive agreement, but 'I've never been able to' is a negative statement requiring 'neither/nor'
'Neither do I' uses the present simple auxiliary 'do', but the original statement uses the present perfect auxiliary 'have' with 'never been able'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: 'David said he might apply for the management position if the salary was high enough.' '___, but I decided the extra responsibility wasn't worth it in the end.'
distractor_rationale: 'So may I' uses the present modal 'may', but the original statement is set in reported speech with past modal 'might', which must be matched in the response
'So did I' uses the past simple auxiliary 'did', but the original statement uses the modal 'might', not a main verb in past simple
'So would I' uses the modal 'would', which expresses a different meaning (hypothetical certainty) rather than the possibility expressed by 'might' in the original

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria loves Italian food, and so I do.
content.error_sentence: Maria loves Italian food, and so I do.
content.corrected_sentence: Maria loves Italian food, and so do I.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom can't swim very well, and neither I can.
content.error_sentence: Tom can't swim very well, and neither I can.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom can't swim very well, and neither can I.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: James finished the report early yesterday, and so do I.
content.error_sentence: James finished the report early yesterday, and so do I.
content.corrected_sentence: James finished the report early yesterday, and so did I.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna doesn't enjoy working late, and so doesn't David.
content.error_sentence: Anna doesn't enjoy working late, and so doesn't David.
content.corrected_sentence: Anna doesn't enjoy working late, and neither does David.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara had already eaten lunch when they arrived, and so had either Lisa.
content.error_sentence: Sara had already eaten lunch when they arrived, and so had either Lisa.
content.corrected_sentence: Sara had already eaten lunch when they arrived, and so had Lisa.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: David has been feeling tired all week because of the extra shifts, and neither has Mark.
content.error_sentence: David has been feeling tired all week because of the extra shifts, and neither has Mark.
content.corrected_sentence: David has been feeling tired all week because of the extra shifts, and so has Mark.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa couldn't find a seat on the train this morning, and neither did Mark.
content.error_sentence: Lisa couldn't find a seat on the train this morning, and neither did Mark.
content.corrected_sentence: Lisa couldn't find a seat on the train this morning, and neither could Mark.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We were told that the meeting had been cancelled, and so were either our colleagues in the other office.
content.error_sentence: We were told that the meeting had been cancelled, and so were either our colleagues in the other office.
content.corrected_sentence: We were told that the meeting had been cancelled, and so were our colleagues in the other office.
